finding: Agents form conclusive judgments and reach decision thresholds far earlier in evidence sequences than they predict they will; the capacity to integrate accumulated information occurs faster than anticipated, even when observers have full knowledge of the evidence domain.

Physiological arousal to valenced information shows consistent asymmetry cross-nationally: negative stimuli elicit greater sympathetic activation than positive stimuli, but individual variation is high and not systematically explained by national or cultural context.

Social group membership encoding is not an automatic primitive but a flexible inference mechanism driven by coalitional cues; when behavioral markers predict group allegiances better than visual appearance, the importance of appearance-based categorization diminishes despite lifetime conditioning.

Lay judgments of free will and moral responsibility do not align with incompatibilist predictions; most people attribute free will and responsibility to agents in deterministic scenarios, challenging philosophers' claims that folk intuitions naturally support incompatibilism.

Folk attributions of moral belief vary with the directness of motivation coupling and explanatory context; people apply internalist constraints (requiring motivation) more to belief judgments than understanding, and relax constraints when lack of motivation is explained by temporary factors.

Interruptions of continuous positive experiences can increase net enjoyment by disrupting hedonic adaptation; this effect is bounded by viewers' propensity to adapt and the program's tendency to elicit adaptation, reversing intuitive predictions about uninterrupted experiences.
